item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be sourced, and thus is assumed to be non-complian with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). Thus, it has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: very interesting, the stuff with the dreamer reminds me a bit of morrowind

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: IMO, the AWCY reference needs to be removed. Takes the article from interesting and mysterious to silly and meaningless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorts upthread pretty much encapsulates my feelings, except with how the original peice never really chamred me in the first place. I would downvote, but I can see why other people would like it, so I'll leave it at a novote.
Very poignant. I like it a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like your analysis. Good points.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My favorite part of this isn't the last weird thing the guy in the interview says. It's the one before, about vanquishing the dreamer.

It's perfectly reasonable to conclude that the subjects display elevated EEG readings while their bodies are wasting away because they're still considering and reacting to the painting to the exclusion of everything else.

+ But what if it's more than that?
- Let's examine it further.

Given this image that consistently suggests that the viewer has been judged by some higher power and labeled worthless, the viewers express awe over the apparent truthfulness and elegance of the argument and then lie down and die.

But if they're lying down and dying because they agree that they're worthless, why do they display high levels of brain activity while they do it? I don't know about anyone else, but when I feel worthless, I want to shut down. I avoid thinking too hard. I try to go to sleep. These people aren't doing that- they're thinking very hard indeed.

What if the subjects are responding so strongly as to cease all other action… and their response is a rejection of the message? What if, convinced that they're nothing more than part of a dream, they're going into their own dreams to fight the dreamer who categorized them as meaningless? What if they're committing their entire being to saying "no, I do matter, I am real and I have meaning" to a cosmos or creator that apparently disagrees?

On the Foundation's level of observation- observing and testing the object's anomalous properties- this is a piece about the symbiotic relationship between art and viewer. It demonstrates that even the most meaningful artwork appears drab and uninteresting without a viewer to comment on it, and that even the most drab and uninteresting artwork can become incredibly meaningful to the right viewer.

On the individual's level of observation, this is a piece about the adversarial relationship between artist and critic. It evokes the feeling of having the thing that contains your entire existence judged and rejected, and of desperately wanting to change the verdict.

tl;dr - In Soviet AWCY?, article downvotes you.

Definitely cool. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Are we cool yet?"

Dramatic Squirrel

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Echoing FFB on this one. I was a bit torn about the stinger for a second, but after some deliberation I realized this was right up AWCY's alley. It felt like a satirization of what people think art can do, or how art is more dependent on the viewer than it's actual content… The possibilities go on. To prevent a ramble, I'm just gonna say I liked it and I +1'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very enthusiastic upvote from just the last line of the interview. I was thinking, "Meh." until I got to that last line. I could just picture the SOB looking RIGHT at the interviewer and saying that. CREEPY!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It does add that good element of creepiness, being that the painting appears as something completely moving to the viewers although in reality, it's just gray. It's one of those that really sends a shiver down my spine and makes me wonder what it would appear as to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think so. One might say that the point of imbuing a blank canvass with this matter of anomaly which is so moving you will literally cease to care about anything else, could be viewed as a statement that true art relies more on the viewer for context and meaning than it does on the content of the actual work. The phenomenon of any two people reporting completely different paintings is analogous to you and I looking at this work.

You see something pointless, unrelated to a greater message, guilty of name dropping. Whereas I see a piece that definitely demonstrates AWCY's style of subversion through anomalous art. Everyone thinks they're viewing a painting of supreme quality and worth. They all, in fact, think they're viewing a different painting of supreme quality and worth. In truth, it's just grey-on-gray. The audience itself delivers the content and the context to the work entirely.

I think it's a great example of AWCY done well. The last question in the interview is a bit of a silly way to reveal their involvement, but the anomaly and the artwork being described are well within the bounds of a well-constructed AWCY piece.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought the entire point of AWCY? was to use anomalous art to make a point about society or art. The absence of shark one did that really well, but this just seems kind of pointless.

I just feel like this is one where just because it's related to art they slapped AWCY? onto it as a hook so people could go "Oh, I love AWCY lol that's so cool how they're related to this"

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: This means AWCY is very cool indeed!

Congrats Smapti, another +100 under the belt!

*confetti*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup, I've always liked this. This is the only AWCY article that caught me off guard, and I applaud it for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this from the start, and having a picture of the thing helped. However, I, too, simply must comment on the AWCY? stinger at the end. And I will do it like so:

The whole article was like a symphony going through beautiful motions I loved listening to, each note and chord flowing smoothly into the next one and floating me along on a glorious melody that was simply masterful - intricate enough to capture my full attention, and soft and subtle enough to make me try to listen harder so as to make out every tiny sound. Then, at the last second, the whole song suddenly turned fortissimo and I was sent reeling, feeling as though I'd been punched in the gut, and was left wondrously in shock as the last few notes softly carried the piece to a satisfying end.

To put it in more understandable words, I was reading along, very much enjoying the article, and then, as soon as I saw the D-class's last words, I literally stopped reading for a minute and just sat there as my mind slowly put the pieces together and my eyes got wider and wider until I read the note that followed, after which I just sat back and stared at my screen in awe for a few moments before coming to the comments section.

I'm not sure how I feel about AWCY? as a whole - I've only just started seeing them since starting SCP series 2 recently, before that I'd barely even heard of them - but just the way this was presented was utterly masterful. +1, and +20 if I could. Bravo. *applauds*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I totally think the AWCY reference works here.

Mainly because, as I was reading what the D-class was saying right before that last line, I kept thinking: "Man, this is some pretentious artsy meaningless tripe." And then it was the group that's all about pretentious and artsy. Perfect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: are we cool yet :D just like the absence of shark. i'm liking these ones so far.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it's funny, reading through the comments, how many people love the AWCY stinger and how many people think that's the main downside of this article.

I was actually getting ready to downvote when I read the the stinger and scrolled up to hit the +1. It's the unexpected tonal shift that really gets me, rather than the individual elements.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for the Interview Log, even though I didn't like the AWCY punch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What if we're the crazy ones, and the painting sets us free?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The AWCY stinger felt really out of place, like you've got this guy who's progressing through existential torment, awakening and enlightenment at breakneck speed and then, right before he goes catatonic from cosmic analysis paralysis, he turns to the camera and gives us a wink and a pistol-finger.

I dig AWCY, and overall this does seem like one of their gigs, but where, when and how the reveal is done just totally ground the scene to a halt in my head.

Everything save that is solid, though. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's being upvoted because it's good. People don't get upvotes because they're "celebrities," it's the other way around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, Lireci -as an aside, if you check, this was written when Smapti wasn't a celebrity yet. He went into a hyperdrive much much later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is X The Macarena?
As an aside, I hate this one too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A cliché isn't something you can't write, but it's something that's been done plenty. As much as I like Smapti it has nothing to do with how I vote on an article, if the article sucks then it sucks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is this just being upvoted because the author is a celebrity? The entire content of the article can be summarized as cliches #6, #11, and AWCY.

It's an X that does X really well and makes you want to do X with it and makes you want to get other people to do X with it too. Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll have you know that I have a 4th wall moment from 11:02am to 11:26am every weekday.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems that he had a fourth wall moment there in that interview. They really don't exist except in imagination.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually love this one, because it's the *exact same idea I had at one point*, I was just never able to make it work to my own satisfaction.

The AWCY neither adds nor retracts from this IMO, and since I was gonna upvote it anyway..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is just… not doing anything for me, and the bit at the end seems dreadfully forced.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neither do I, but at this stage I think that's the point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i'm fairly neutral toward AWCY, but i also dont have too much of a problem with "things what make you crazy" so long as the crazy is a type that makes SENSE. this makes sense, the effects match what you could expect from something that makes you see every increasing levels of complexity and provokes deep uncontrolled thoughts.

i also would really REALLY like it if this were what created the AWCY movement rather than something that AWCY made themselves. enough so that unless something comes out to explicitly state how the group came about, that's what my headcanon is going to be. a bunch of art freaks saw this and somehow managed to survive, came out the other end of it enlightened, and decided to change the world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interview log and the AWCY thing are the best part of this article. The rest is really just boring, it's a painting what makes you go crazy and die. I'd say either make the effects more interesting or shorten the description because it's unnecessarily long.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like AWCY and having them used as the zinger really took all my enjoyment out of the piece, which is otherwise great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Smapti, are you and Tanhony part of the same machine or something? Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, I upvoted once I got to Stendhal syndrome- this SCP is a perfect example of all the creepy things you can find if you do some research. And then I got to the last line and I wanted to upvote again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really really love AWCY. This is a good SCP, and the interview seals the deal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted because I love AWCY.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will admit, I wasn't going to upvote until I read that last line. I'd been waiting to see if AWCY was in any way involved, and I almost feel disappointed that I was right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last line lost my upvote. Not saying you should take it out - clearly I'm in the minority. But I just don't get Are We Cool Yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was kind of boring until the interview, then it took off, Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wasn't going to vote until I read his last question. I'm guessing you'll see a lot more +1s because of that last line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another upvote almost entirely from the last interview line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Son of a bitch you got me with that last line. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I saw this comment, and the one above about "are we cool yet". I was still not prepared. Fantastic way to end the interview - i'm upvoting for that reason alone. Though i thought the rest of it was pretty good, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That last line in the interview really got me. Smapti, you are a machine. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the Are We Cool Yet bit. I could actually see this being the source of that group, rather than a product of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it a painting what makes you crazy? Yeah, I s'pose. Do I still love it? …yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As said in chat, nothing wrong with this technically. There's just not enough here to elevate this above Painting What Make You Crazy.
